narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, based on the story your background tells. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 1, the lowest rung in your school, which painted the strongest picture of struggles that often carry forward to bar challenges. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, showing those early hurdles weren't just a one-off but a pattern the model weighed heavily toward predicting failure. Your LSAT score of 30 added to that narrative, signaling to the model a tougher climb in the rigorous demands of legal exams ahead. Layered on top was your low family income, which the model linked to extra barriers many face in sustaining the intense prep needed without broader support. Your background as a Black female student also influenced the outlook, reflecting systemic patterns the model has learned from past outcomes that pulled toward this risk. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 and full-time enrollment status wove in, suggesting the model foresaw divided focus or foundational gaps amplifying the odds. Together, these elements added up to a 56% predicted chance of not passing the bar, the threshold we use to protect spots for those with smoother projected paths. It's not a judgment on your potential or spirit—far from it—but a data-driven snapshot urging you to build those academic deciles higher, perhaps retake the LSAT, or seek resources tailored to your situation. You've got resilience in your story already; with targeted steps, you can rewrite this chapter and reapply stronger. We're rooting for you.
